title: Beyond the AI Hype: A Software Engineer’s Perspective
author: Fernando Abolafio
text:
Beyond the AI Hype: A Software Engineer’s Perspective
How focusing on timeless fundamentals can keep you grounded, productive, and ready for what’s next
I’ve found myself anxious, short breath strokes, fingers eager to scroll onto the next post. To the next AI prototype built in 5 minutes, the 5 reasons on how AI will replace Software Engineers, the 5 new tools that will get me ahead of the AI game, the 5 next big business opportunities enabled by AI. Even the amount of this acronym ‘AI’ in this paragraph makes me annoyed.
And here is the irony, I am building an AI-powered startup. And I love how this technology can be used to deliver novel product to users, I like learning about it. But the flow of information in social media or anywhere is a lot to take in.
What I’ve seen is that when I first started going deep into reading and learning about AI, every new piece would click onto the other, and I’ll be amazed by the possibilities and by the new layers of learning it unblocked. But there was a point where It seemed as I was the sole noob in a world of only experts.
But hey, wait. I’ve been working with it daily. Suffering through the pains of building real software with it. And still I felt like lagging behind? Then it hit me, it can’t be right.
I started poking at some of the profiles, lots of people who haven’t written a single line of code. Plenty of mass content generators. Guess what? It sells.
Here’s a view for you
About ~10 years ago React was just emerging. There was a lot of other frameworks people were trying out and hyped about (there still is). However, React as many others have changed a lot since then. If you were trying to be ahead of the game, you’d be exhausted and postponing doing your best work on delivering true to value to users.
Focusing on the principles of Web, good software design, and the basics of it such as HTML/JS/CSS would likely have given you a better payoff. Those are more stable technologies and you can transfer them across frameworks.
This is similar to how AI frameworks and tools are right now. They are very much fresh and in movement, it’ll take a few years to make some sense of it and to know who are the truly winners we should pay attention to.
Staying close to good software practices: design, test, architecture— just as before is the best strategy I can see. They are invaluable and timeless skills that can be transferred from one language to another, one framework to another.
Shouldn’t you learn AI then?
Yes. Just like any other technology, I’d say. I lost count how many times I wanted to learn more about database indexes and query optimizations or all the different types of joins one can do. But I never prioritized that because it wasn’t central to delivering value through the piece of code I was working on. So I’d ‘snack’ some learnings whenever I could and dig deeper when needed for certain projects.
I see AI in a similar light. But, I am guilty. I’ve been way deeper into learning about AI than many of you who are in a full-time job now where the priorities of the company don’t necessarily align with you catching up with AI. But here’s the funny part of it: what I struggle the most about working with AI, isn’t so much the quirks, prompt engineering and all the newness that comes to it.
What is challenging is the day-to-day challenges of software. Think of RAG, 80% of the work is setting up databases to retrieve data for the LLM with ease. Sorry, but that ain’t new. I’m still doing my good old CRUDing for that. Streaming messages from the server to the app: I still need to think about state management, handling failures, and placing the damn button at the right place(!!).
What about AI replacing developers?
I don’t know, it might. Who knows. Truth is, we (as SWEs) have been through many hype cycles of where we have been doomed and what truly happens was a leap in the abstraction level. Think of close-to-metal languages such as Assembly to C to Objected-oriented Programming. We’ve been given the opportunity to code at higher levels of abstractions and letting the compiler deal with the translation to machine language.
I do see this as another abstraction leap. The language of software isn’t just the raw syntax. It’s about expressing business logic, human relations, and in a ever-evolving system. This last part is the key for me: ever-evolving. Meaning, things are changing all the time and it has an impact on the software. And to this moment, the only way to perceive change in the world, requires you to be part of the world, to be embodied and participating in it. And, from this point of view, translate this flux of rules into something that a machine can be part of, and collaborate with us humans.
Here is the key piece that has been missing: software isn’t about writing for a machine understanding. It’s about creating something for humans. And, I’m positive that we can leverage this new technology to help us in that mission.
Wrapping it up
If you’re just getting started into coding or are a seasoned developer, I beg that you keep your hopes and your craft at a stellar level. We’ll get through this with an even better perspective. Being a SWE has never felt so powerful, just think the amount of things you can do. Things are moving fast and trying to make too much sense of it now is wasteful. Delivering great product is not. And that is the focus I wish for you and myself: protect your cognitive load from external noise and direct that towards a good life and good products.
Extra: If you’re interested in a very sober and FOMO averse view of LLMs, I recommend the snippet of this interview of Grady Booch, co-creator of UML by Gergely Orosz on his view of LLMs:
